critic_raw:
## Analysis of GNN-PPI Methodology

### Issue Group 1: The GNN's access to test-time protein identity constitutes structural leakage under novel-protein settings

**Claim:** GNN-PPI improves inter-novel-protein interaction prediction by leveraging graph structure (neighbor aggregation over the protein interaction graph).

**Why this might be wrong:** A GNN operates by aggregating features from a node's neighborhood in the protein interaction graph. For a protein that is "novel" (unseen during training), its edges in the interaction graph must either (a) not exist at all at inference time, meaning it has no neighbors to aggregate from and the GNN degenerates to a featureless node embedding or sequence-only encoding, or (b) exist at inference time, meaning the test interactions are embedded into the graph before inference — which is leakage, because the existence of an edge to a test node is itself an interaction label. 

If option (b) is what the implementation does (adding all protein nodes including test proteins to the graph, then running message passing), then the GNN can propagate information from known interactions of a novel protein's neighbors back into the novel protein's representation. This means the graph structure encodes information about test proteins derived from training interactions with their neighbors, which is a form of transductive leakage — the model "sees" structural context of novel proteins that an inductive deployment scenario would not permit.

If option (a) is what is done, then for truly novel proteins the GNN provides no benefit over sequence-only encoding, and any improvement claimed for the inter-novel-protein setting must come from something else (e.g., better sequence encoder or training procedure), undermining the core architectural claim.

**Evidence:** Examine whether the full protein interaction graph (including test proteins and their edges) is constructed before the train/test split inference step or strictly after. Check whether novel proteins receive any neighborhood-derived embeddings at test time. An ablation comparing GNN-PPI with neighborhood aggregation disabled (i.e., 0-hop / node feature only) on inter-novel-protein interactions would directly test whether the structural component contributes anything in the truly novel setting.

---

### Issue Group 2: BFS/DFS splitting does not cleanly isolate inter-novel-protein interactions from partially-novel ones

**Claim:** The BFS/DFS graph-based splits provide a rigorous evaluation of inter-novel-protein generalization.

**Why this might be wrong:** BFS and DFS traversals of a protein interaction graph will naturally carve out structurally cohesive subgraphs for test sets, but these traversals do not guarantee that all test interactions are of the inter-novel-protein type (both proteins unseen). Many test interactions will involve one protein seen during training (intra-novel or semi-novel) and one unseen protein. If the reported micro-F1 for the BFS/DFS splits aggregates over all interaction types — including semi-novel ones — then the metric dilutes the inter-novel-protein signal. A model could achieve high aggregate performance on BFS/DFS splits by excelling at semi-novel interactions while performing poorly on truly inter-novel cases.

Furthermore, BFS from a hub protein will enrich test sets with highly connected proteins (hubs), while DFS will produce chains of lower-degree proteins. These topological biases produce systematically different test distributions, making it unclear whether observed performance differences between BFS and DFS reflect model quality or artifacts of which protein types end up in each test set.

**Evidence:** Report micro-F1 separately for (1) both proteins novel, (2) one protein novel, (3) neither novel, within each split strategy. Compare degree distributions of test proteins under BFS vs. DFS vs. random to see whether the splits sample topologically representative proteins or biased subsets.

---

### Issue Group 3: Micro-F1 on a multi-label interaction type task is dominated by frequent classes

**Claim:** Micro-F1 is an appropriate main metric for multi-type PPI prediction.

**Why this might be wrong:** Micro-F1 aggregates true positives, false positives, and false negatives across all interaction types before computing F1. In PPI datasets, interaction types are typically heavily imbalanced (e.g., physical binding is far more common than specific regulatory interactions). A model that achieves high recall on the dominant class while failing on rare classes will earn a high micro-F1. Since the paper's central claim is about generalization to novel proteins — a setting where rare interaction types may be even harder to predict — a micro-F1 dominated by frequent classes could mask systematic failure on the biologically interesting rare types.

**Evidence:** Report macro-F1 and per-class F1 alongside micro-F1. Check whether GNN-PPI's gains over PIPR are concentrated in the most frequent interaction classes. If macro-F1 shows no improvement or degradation while micro-F1 improves, the headline claim is undermined.

---

### Issue Group 4: Comparison baselines may not be run in the same inductive setting

**Claim:** GNN-PPI outperforms PIPR, DNN-PPI, and DPPI especially on inter-novel-protein interactions.

**Why this might be wrong:** PIPR and DPPI are sequence-based models that are inherently inductive — they can predict for any protein given its sequence. GNN-PPI, as a graph-based model, may be run in a transductive or semi-transductive regime (graph constructed over all proteins, weights learned on training edges). If baselines are forced into a strict inductive setup (only training protein sequences and labels available) while GNN-PPI benefits from graph-level transduction, the comparison is not apples-to-apples. The GNN-PPI advantage may reflect access to test-set structural context rather than a better inductive learning mechanism.

**Evidence:** Verify whether baselines are given identical input information to GNN-PPI at inference time. Specifically, do the baselines have access to the same set of proteins (and their features) that GNN-PPI uses for graph construction? An experiment in which GNN-PPI is forced into a strict inductive regime (no edges involving test proteins visible during inference) would test whether its advantage persists under fair conditions.

---

### Issue Group 5: Dataset contamination between SHS27k/SHS148k and STRING

**Claim:** Cross-dataset experiments demonstrate generalization.

**Why this might be wrong:** SHS27k and SHS148k are reported to be subsets or derived from STRING. If the train set for a model trained on SHS148k shares proteins with the STRING test set (because STRING is the superset), then cross-dataset evaluation is not evaluating generalization to genuinely novel proteins — it is evaluating interpolation within a known protein universe. This would inflate apparent cross-dataset generalization performance.

**Evidence:** Report the overlap in protein identifiers between training sets drawn from SHS27k/SHS148k and the test set drawn from STRING. If overlap is substantial (>10% of test proteins appear in training), the cross-dataset claim is not supported.

---

### Summary

The most significant issue is Issue Group 1: the GNN's core architectural mechanism (neighborhood aggregation) is either leaking test-set graph structure into novel protein representations (undermining the evaluation) or is inactive for novel proteins (undermining the architectural claim). This is a precondition for the central hypothesis to hold, and the paper does not appear to address it explicitly. Issue Groups 2 and 4 are also substantive and concern whether the evaluation framework actually isolates the claimed setting and whether comparisons are fair.
all_issues_raised: GNN neighborhood aggregation for novel proteins either leaks test-set graph structure (if test edges are visible during inference) or provides no structural benefit (if they are not), undermining the core architectural claim in either case.

BFS/DFS splits aggregate semi-novel and inter-novel interactions in the reported micro-F1, diluting the signal for truly inter-novel pairs and introducing topological biases from hub vs. chain traversal patterns.
Micro-F1 is dominated by frequent interaction classes, potentially masking systematic failure on rare types that are disproportionately hard under the inter-novel-protein setting.

Baselines (PIPR, DPPI) may be evaluated in a stricter inductive regime than GNN-PPI, which may benefit from transductive graph construction over all proteins including test proteins, making the comparison unfair.
SHS27k and SHS148k are derived from STRING, so cross-dataset experiments likely have substantial protein-level overlap between training and test sets, inflating apparent cross-dataset generalization.

GNN message passing on novel proteins either leaks test-time graph information (transductive inference) or degenerates to a sequence-only method, undermining the claimed architectural advantage for inter-novel-protein interactions.
BFS/DFS splits systematically push low-degree peripheral proteins into the test set, confounding structural peripherality with novelty and making INP gains uninterpretable.

Protein-based splits applied to multi-type interactions can cause rare interaction types to be unevenly distributed across train/test, while micro-F1 aggregation obscures per-type generalization failure.
Micro-F1 is dominated by common interaction types; reported INP gains may reflect improvement only on dominant types rather than broad multi-type generalization.
Comparison with sequence-only baselines like PIPR does not control for the additional input modality (graph edges), conflating input information advantage with architectural advantage.

The GNN mechanism presupposes that PPI graph neighborhood structure encodes interaction-type-specific information transferable to novel proteins, a prerequisite that is not verified and may not hold given the noisy, inferred nature of STRING edges.

The GNN uses the interaction graph for message passing at inference time, which may include novel test-protein nodes and their edges, creating transductive leakage that undermines the inter-novel-protein evaluation.
BFS/DFS splits control graph topology but not sequence homology, so 'novel' test proteins may be highly similar to training proteins, making the split less challenging than claimed.
Micro-F1 on imbalanced multi-type interaction data is dominated by frequent interaction types, potentially concealing poor performance on rare types that constitute the harder generalization cases.
GNN-PPI is compared against sequence-only baselines without controlling for the additional graph data it receives, conflating architectural advantage with information advantage.
The inter-novel-protein test subset is likely small and no confidence intervals or statistical tests are reported, making the claim of 'particularly pronounced' gains unreliable.

Novel-protein node representations may be computed via GNN neighborhood aggregation over training proteins even when the protein is nominally 'unseen,' constituting a silent misconfiguration of the inductive evaluation.

## Analysis of GNN-PPI Methodology

### Issue Group 1: The GNN's access to test-time protein identity constitutes structural leakage under novel-protein settings

**Claim:** GNN-PPI improves inter-novel-protein interaction prediction by leveraging graph structure (neighbor aggregation over the protein interaction graph).

**Why this might be wrong:** A GNN operates by aggregating features from a node's neighborhood in the protein interaction graph. If option (b) is what the implementation does (adding all protein nodes including test proteins to the graph, then running message passing), then the GNN can propagate information from known interactions of a novel protein's neighbors back into the novel protein's representation. This means the graph structure encodes information about test proteins derived from training interactions with their neighbors, which is a form of transductive leakage — the model "sees" structural context of novel proteins that an inductive deployment scenario would not permit.

If option (a) is what is done, then for truly novel proteins the GNN provides no benefit over sequence-only encoding, and any improvement claimed for the inter-novel-protein setting must come from something else (e.g., better sequence encoder or training procedure), undermining the core architectural claim.

---

### Issue Group 2: BFS/DFS splitting does not cleanly isolate inter-novel-protein interactions from partially-novel ones

**Claim:** The BFS/DFS graph-based splits provide a rigorous evaluation of inter-novel-protein generalization.

**Why this might be wrong:** BFS and DFS traversals of a protein interaction graph will naturally carve out structurally cohesive subgraphs for test sets, but these traversals do not guarantee that all test interactions are of the inter-novel-protein type (both proteins unseen). A model could achieve high aggregate performance on BFS/DFS splits by excelling at semi-novel interactions while performing poorly on truly inter-novel cases.

Furthermore, BFS from a hub protein will enrich test sets with highly connected proteins (hubs), while DFS will produce chains of lower-degree proteins. Compare degree distributions of test proteins under BFS vs. DFS vs. random to see whether the splits sample topologically representative proteins or biased subsets.

---

### Issue Group 3: Micro-F1 on a multi-label interaction type task is dominated by frequent classes

**Claim:** Micro-F1 is an appropriate main metric for multi-type PPI prediction.

**Why this might be wrong:** Micro-F1 aggregates true positives, false positives, and false negatives across all interaction types before computing F1. In PPI datasets, interaction types are typically heavily imbalanced (e.g., physical binding is far more common than specific regulatory interactions). A model that achieves high recall on the dominant class while failing on rare classes will earn a high micro-F1. Since the paper's central claim is about generalization to novel proteins — a setting where rare interaction types may be even harder to predict — a micro-F1 dominated by frequent classes could mask systematic failure on the biologically interesting rare types.

**Evidence:** Report macro-F1 and per-class F1 alongside micro-F1. Check whether GNN-PPI's gains over PIPR are concentrated in the most frequent interaction classes. If macro-F1 shows no improvement or degradation while micro-F1 improves, the headline claim is undermined.

---

### Issue Group 4: Comparison baselines may not be run in the same inductive setting

**Claim:** GNN-PPI outperforms PIPR, DNN-PPI, and DPPI especially on inter-novel-protein interactions.

**Why this might be wrong:** PIPR and DPPI are sequence-based models that are inherently inductive — they can predict for any protein given its sequence. GNN-PPI, as a graph-based model, may be run in a transductive or semi-transductive regime (graph constructed over all proteins, weights learned on training edges). If baselines are forced into a strict inductive setup (only training protein sequences and labels available) while GNN-PPI benefits from graph-level transduction, the comparison is not apples-to-apples. The GNN-PPI advantage may reflect access to test-set structural context rather than a better inductive learning mechanism.

**Evidence:** Verify whether baselines are given identical input information to GNN-PPI at inference time. Specifically, do the baselines have access to the same set of proteins (and their features) that GNN-PPI uses for graph construction? An experiment in which GNN-PPI is forced into a strict inductive regime (no edges involving test proteins visible during inference) would test whether its advantage persists under fair conditions.

---

### Issue Group 5: Dataset contamination between SHS27k/SHS148k and STRING

**Claim:** Cross-dataset experiments demonstrate generalization.

**Why this might be wrong:** SHS27k and SHS148k are reported to be subsets or derived from STRING. If the train set for a model trained on SHS148k shares proteins with the STRING test set (because STRING is the superset), then cross-dataset evaluation is not evaluating generalization to genuinely novel proteins — it is evaluating interpolation within a known protein universe. assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Analysis of GNN-PPI Methodology

### Group 1: Information Leakage Through Graph Structure

**Issue 1: GNN message passing during inference implicitly uses test protein neighborhoods**

The specific claim: GNN-PPI improves inter-novel-protein (INP) prediction by incorporating relational structure from the protein interaction graph, where a novel protein at test time can aggregate information from its known neighbors.

Why it might be wrong: This is the central contradiction. For a protein to be genuinely "novel" (unseen during training), it must appear only in test interactions. But if the GNN operates on the full interaction graph at inference time — or if edges involving novel proteins are included in the graph used for message passing — then information about the novel protein's neighborhood is implicitly fed into the prediction. This is not generalization; it is transductive inference. Conversely, if novel proteins are strictly excluded from the message-passing graph, then they have no node embeddings at all, and the GNN degenerates to a sequence-based method for this subset, eliminating the claimed architectural advantage precisely where it is claimed to matter most.

What constitutes evidence: The paper should explicitly describe (a) whether novel-protein nodes are present in the message-passing graph at test time, (b) whether edges incident to novel proteins are included, and (c) what node initialization is used for novel protein nodes. An ablation comparing GNN-PPI on INP interactions with and without neighbor aggregation for novel nodes would directly test whether the structural signal is real generalization or transductive leakage.

---

### Group 2: Split Construction Validity

**Issue 2: BFS/DFS splits may not produce genuine distribution shift for the claimed purpose**

The specific claim: BFS and DFS graph-based splits control how proteins distribute between train and test, yielding a more realistic assessment of generalization to novel proteins.

Why it might be wrong: BFS and DFS traversal order determines which proteins end up in which partition, but neither guarantees that the resulting split reflects any biologically meaningful difference between "known" and "novel" proteins. The topology of the PPI graph (hub proteins, interaction density) will systematically bias which proteins land in train vs. test: high-degree hub proteins will almost always end up in the training set under BFS (since they are explored early), while low-degree peripheral proteins end up in test. This creates a confound: GNN-PPI may perform well on INP interactions not because it generalizes to novel proteins, but because test proteins are structurally peripheral and their interactions are easier to predict (or easier to dismiss as non-interacting). The split design would then be measuring "predict interactions of peripheral proteins" rather than "generalize to truly unseen proteins."

What constitutes evidence: A degree-stratified analysis of test proteins — comparing performance on high-degree vs. low-degree novel proteins within the BFS/DFS splits — would reveal whether the gains are concentrated among structurally peripheral proteins. Additionally, comparing the degree distribution of train vs. test proteins under BFS/DFS vs. random splits would characterize the actual distributional shift being tested.

**Issue 3: Label leakage through shared interaction types across splits**

The specific claim: The evaluation is multi-type PPI prediction and the splits are defined over proteins, not interaction types.

Why it might be wrong: If some interaction types are rare but the split is protein-based, rare interaction types may have most of their examples concentrated in train or in test by chance, making per-type performance highly variable and micro-F1 aggregates misleading. Specifically, if rare types appear predominantly in training, the model can effectively learn those labels transductively; if they appear predominantly in test, the model is evaluated on types it has barely seen. Micro-F1 collapses this variation and can report high overall performance driven entirely by majority interaction types.

What constitutes evidence: Per-type F1 breakdown across splits, and a comparison of interaction type frequency distributions between train and test partitions, would reveal whether the aggregated micro-F1 is masking type-level generalization failure.

---

### Group 3: Metric and Baseline Comparison Validity

**Issue 4: Micro-F1 on an imbalanced multi-label problem is dominated by common interaction types**

The specific claim: Micro-F1 is used as the main metric and GNN-PPI's gains are "particularly pronounced" for INP interactions.

Why it might be wrong: Micro-F1 weights each instance equally, so a model that perfectly predicts the most common interaction types and fails entirely on rare types will score very highly. In PPI datasets with highly skewed type distributions (binding, catalysis, reaction, etc.), the micro-F1 improvement could be entirely attributable to better calibration on the top-1 or top-2 dominant types, with no meaningful improvement on rare types. The claim about INP interaction gains would then be driven by INP interactions that happen to involve the common types rather than genuine cross-type generalization.

What constitutes evidence: Macro-F1 or per-class F1 reported alongside micro-F1. If macro-F1 gains are substantially smaller than micro-F1 gains, the conclusion about broad INP improvement should be revised.

**Issue 5: Baselines like PIPR operate on sequence only; the comparison is not controlled for input modality**

The specific claim: GNN-PPI outperforms PIPR and other baselines, demonstrating the value of graph-structural information.

Why it might be wrong: PIPR uses sequence-only input. GNN-PPI uses sequence plus graph structure. Attributing the performance difference to the GNN architecture conflates the effect of additional input information (graph edges) with the effect of the model architecture. For INP interactions specifically, if the graph connectivity gives the model any information about which proteins tend to co-interact (even without direct sequence comparison), this is an input advantage, not an architectural advantage. The baseline should include a model that uses the same graph-structural features without the GNN (e.g., degree, clustering coefficient, or embedding from a simpler graph method) to isolate the contribution of the GNN mechanism.

What constitutes evidence: An ablation in which GNN-PPI's graph input is replaced with handcrafted graph features fed to the same MLP head would isolate whether the GNN architecture itself is necessary, or whether any graph-aware input improvement would suffice.

---

### Group 4: Prerequisite Assumptions for the GNN Mechanism

**Issue 6: The GNN mechanism assumes that interaction type correlates with graph neighborhood structure in a learnable way**

The specific claim: Graph-structural correlations between proteins enable better prediction of interaction types for novel proteins.

Why it might be wrong: For the GNN to help, the neighborhood of a protein in the PPI graph must carry information about what interaction types that protein can participate in — beyond what the protein's own sequence encodes. This is a non-trivial assumption. PPI graph edges in datasets like STRING include edges of unknown or inferred type; using these edges for message passing mixes supervised signal (known typed interactions) with noisy inferred signal. If the graph structure does not encode interaction-type-specific information in a way that is consistent across novel proteins, the GNN aggregation adds noise rather than signal, and the performance on INP interactions would reflect memorization of the training graph topology rather than generalization.

What constitutes evidence: A test of whether GNN-PPI's node embeddings cluster by interaction type preference (i.e., proteins that predominantly participate in binding interactions should cluster together) would confirm that the graph structure encodes the necessary signal. Alternatively, an experiment on a shuffled graph (edges randomized, topology preserved) would show whether the specific graph connectivity, as opposed to node degree statistics, is responsible for the gains.

issues_raised: The GNN uses the interaction graph for message passing at inference time, which may include novel test-protein nodes and their edges, creating transductive leakage that undermines the inter-novel-protein evaluation.
BFS/DFS splits control graph topology but not sequence homology, so 'novel' test proteins may be highly similar to training proteins, making the split less challenging than claimed.
Micro-F1 on imbalanced multi-type interaction data is dominated by frequent interaction types, potentially concealing poor performance on rare types that constitute the harder generalization cases.
GNN-PPI is compared against sequence-only baselines without controlling for the additional graph data it receives, conflating architectural advantage with information advantage.
The inter-novel-protein test subset is likely small and no confidence intervals or statistical tests are reported, making the claim of 'particularly pronounced' gains unreliable.

Novel-protein node representations may be computed via GNN neighborhood aggregation over training proteins even when the protein is nominally 'unseen,' constituting a silent misconfiguration of the inductive evaluation.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
## Analysis of GNN-PPI Methodology

### Issue Group 1: Graph Leakage — The Core Validity Problem

**Issue 1: GNN-PPI uses the interaction graph as input, but the test interactions are edges in that same graph**

The specific claim: GNN-PPI improves performance on inter-novel-protein interactions by leveraging relational structure in the protein interaction graph during inference.

Why this is wrong: The protein interaction graph that serves as the GNN's message-passing substrate is built from the same dataset being evaluated. If test-set interactions (or even their protein nodes) are present in the graph used for neighborhood aggregation during inference, then the GNN has access to relational signal that directly involves the test targets. Even if the edge labels are withheld, the graph topology encodes co-occurrence structure. A novel protein defined as "unseen during training" may still appear as a node in the graph if the graph was constructed from the full dataset before splitting. This is a form of transductive leakage: the model sees the protein's neighborhood during message passing even though it nominally wasn't trained on that protein's interactions.

What would constitute evidence: An ablation where (a) the interaction graph is restricted strictly to training proteins and their interactions, and (b) performance is re-measured. If GNN-PPI's advantage over sequence-only baselines like PIPR substantially narrows or disappears when the graph is so restricted, the improvement was attributable to graph leakage rather than genuine generalization. The paper should report whether novel-protein nodes are present in the inference-time graph.

---

### Issue Group 2: Evaluation Framework Design

**Issue 2: BFS/DFS splits control graph topology but not sequence similarity**

The specific claim: BFS and DFS splits provide a more realistic assessment of generalization to novel proteins.

Why this might be wrong: Graph-based splits ensure that certain proteins are withheld from training edges, but they do not control for sequence homology. If a "novel" test protein is highly homologous to a training protein, a sequence-based model like PIPR benefits from that homology implicitly through sequence encoding, while a GNN also benefits through the interaction graph structure of homologs. The split strategy does not decorrelate these two sources of signal. True novelty requires both topological and sequence-level separation. Without a homology-controlled split (e.g., 30% or 40% sequence identity cutoff as is standard in structural bioinformatics), the inter-novel-protein setting may not be as challenging as claimed.

What would constitute evidence: Measuring sequence identity between test-set novel proteins and their nearest training-set neighbors. If the distribution of max sequence identities is concentrated above 40%, the "novel" proteins are not genuinely novel in the biological sense. An additional split using sequence-identity-based clustering (e.g., MMseqs2) would provide a cleaner test.

**Issue 3: Micro-F1 on an imbalanced multi-label interaction type task may inflate apparent performance**

The specific claim: Micro-F1 is a valid primary metric for multi-type PPI prediction.

Why this might be wrong: Multi-type PPI datasets are typically highly imbalanced — a few interaction types dominate. Micro-F1 aggregates across all type instances, meaning performance on rare interaction types (which are arguably the harder and more scientifically interesting cases) is swamped by performance on frequent types. A model that perfectly classifies the dominant type while failing entirely on rare types could still report high micro-F1. This matters especially for inter-novel-protein interactions, where rare interaction types may be disproportionately represented among the missed predictions.

What would constitute evidence: Reporting macro-F1 or per-class F1 alongside micro-F1. If the gap between micro-F1 and macro-F1 is large for GNN-PPI on inter-novel-protein interactions, the headline improvement is concentrated in easy/common interaction types.

---

### Issue Group 3: Baseline Comparability

**Issue 4: Baselines like PIPR are sequence-only, making the comparison asymmetric in information access**

The specific claim: GNN-PPI outperforms PIPR and other baselines, demonstrating the value of graph-relational structure.

Why this might be wrong: PIPR and similar baselines operate purely on protein sequences. GNN-PPI additionally consumes the interaction graph. This is not a controlled comparison of modeling architecture — it is a comparison of modeling architecture plus additional input data. The correct ablation is a version of GNN-PPI that uses graph structure but no sequence features, and a version of PIPR augmented with network features (e.g., graph embeddings like node2vec or DeepWalk concatenated to sequence embeddings). Without this, the performance gap cannot be attributed to the GNN architecture per se versus simply having more input information.

What would constitute evidence: An ablation (a) GNN with no sequence features, (b) sequence-only model augmented with graph embeddings. If (b) matches or exceeds full GNN-PPI, the GNN architecture is not doing the work — the graph data is.

**Issue 5: The inter-novel-protein subset is necessarily smaller and its evaluation is noisier**

The specific claim: GNN-PPI gains are "particularly pronounced" for inter-novel-protein interactions.

Why this might be wrong: Under BFS/DFS splits, inter-novel-protein interactions form a subset of the test set. The size of this subset and its class balance are not reported explicitly. If the subset is small, F1 differences between methods can be large in absolute terms due to variance rather than systematic advantage. Without confidence intervals or statistical significance tests on this specific subset, the claim of pronounced improvement is unreliable.

What would constitute evidence: Reporting the number of inter-novel-protein test instances per dataset per split strategy, along with standard deviations across multiple split seeds. If the inter-novel-protein subset contains fewer than a few hundred instances, point estimates of F1 are unreliable.

---

### Issue Group 4: Silent Misconfiguration Risk

**Issue 6: Novel-protein definition may be inconsistently applied across the pipeline**

The specific claim: The evaluation correctly identifies and isolates inter-novel-protein interactions.

Why this might be wrong: The definition of "novel" requires that both proteins in the pair were absent from training. If the implementation identifies novel proteins at the node level using training graph membership but the graph was constructed before splitting (see Issue 1), a protein could be classified as "novel" (no training edges) while still having its node representation updated via GNN message passing from neighboring training proteins. The model effectively sees the protein during inference graph construction. This silent misconfiguration would produce plausible-looking results on easy inter-novel cases (those with strong homologs or graph neighbors) while the claimed generalization mechanism is not actually being tested.

What would constitute evidence: Explicitly stating whether novel-protein nodes are present in the inference-time GNN graph, and whether their embeddings are computed from graph neighborhoods or initialized from sequence features alone. A correct implementation for truly inductive novel-protein prediction would require either (a) retraining with the novel protein included, or (b) using only sequence-derived features for novel proteins without graph aggregation.

---

## Summary

The most fundamental flaw is the potential graph leakage in the transductive/inductive boundary (Issue 1 and Issue 6): if GNN-PPI performs message passing over a graph that includes novel test-protein nodes and their interactions at inference time, the "inter-novel-protein" evaluation is not measuring inductive generalization at all. This would invalidate the central claim. The asymmetric information comparison (Issue 4) is the second-ranking concern because it conflates architectural contribution with data contribution. The remaining issues (homology control, metric choice, subset size) are meaningful but secondary.
